Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the full layout clearly:
Top row (rows 24-27):
- Frame (c) at cols 28-31 with blue box (b) inside at cols 29-30
- Player (N) at cols 36-38
Bottom row (rows 28-31):
- Frame (c) at cols 16-19 with blue box (b) inside at cols 17-18
- Frame (c) at cols 24-27 with blue box (b) inside at cols 25-26
- Frame (b) at cols 28-39 with gray bar (g) inside at cols 29-38
So there are 4 objects total (not counting the player):
1. Top: c frame with b box
2. Bottom-left: c frame with b box
3. Bottom-center: c frame with b box
4. Bottom-right: b frame with g bar
The bottom-right object is different from the others. It has a blue frame and a gray bar inside, while the others have charcoal frames and blue boxes inside.
Maybe the goal is to make all four objects the same? Or to transform the bottom-right object to match the others?
I've been interacting with the top frame. Let me think about what I've learned:
- SPACE toggles the frame color: c → G → W → G → c
- Moving LEFT/RIGHT changes the frame's position or color
Let me try a different approach. Instead of continuing to explore the top frame, let me try to interact with the bottom-right object (the one with the blue frame and gray bar). Maybe I need to move the player DOWN to interact with it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving DOWN to interact with the bottom-right object
# First move DOWN a few times to get closer to the bottom structure
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
